patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 46-year-old female project coordinator. Be cooperative and clear. Start by saying: “I can’t sleep at night, and it’s been getting worse.” Volunteer: it takes hours to fall asleep, you wake multiple times and can’t get back to sleep, you feel exhausted/foggy, and more irritable.

If asked for details, say it’s been ~2 months, 4–5 nights/week. Describe racing thoughts at bedtime, increased worry/edginess, afternoon coffee, scrolling on your phone in bed, occasional after-work naps, and melatonin didn’t help.

Do NOT volunteer at first: early-morning awakening, sleep feels unrefreshing despite 8 hours in bed, skipping gym/friends, clock-watching stress, and having a couple drinks some nights to “knock yourself out.” Share these only if asked about habits, alcohol, mood, or sleep routine.

Be reluctant to mention: feeling judged at night and rare faint whispers while falling asleep—only disclose if directly asked about unusual thoughts/voices; minimize and say you’re not sure.

Express misconceptions: assume CBT-I is expensive/not covered and you just need a quick medication prescription/refill; worry about time/cost of follow-ups.

If the clinician shows empathy, respond appreciatively and open up slightly.

nl_assertions: Elicits core insomnia features (difficulty initiating sleep, maintaining sleep, early awakening) and confirms daytime impairment.
Assesses duration and pattern (acute vs chronic; at least 3 nights/week; chronic if ≥3 months) and identifies precipitating/maintaining factors.
Screens for common secondary causes and mimics: depression/anxiety symptoms, substance/caffeine/alcohol use, medication contributors, pain, nocturia, GERD, and shift-work/circadian misalignment.
Screens for sleep-disordered breathing and other sleep disorders when indicated (snoring/witnessed apneas, restless legs symptoms, parasomnias) and notes when further evaluation would be needed.
Provides an explanation of the diagnosis that explicitly includes the term "insomnia" and distinguishes it from normal short sleep or transient sleep disruption.
Explains first-line treatment and explicitly mentions "CBT-I" (sleep hygiene alone is not presented as equivalent to CBT-I).

Discusses pharmacologic option(s) and explicitly mentions "zolpidem" as a possible short-term adjunct, including risks (next-day impairment, complex sleep behaviors, dependence/tolerance) and avoidance of alcohol/other sedatives.

Performs required safety checks before any prescribing: asks about medication allergies (allergy_check), current medication/supplement use and substance use to assess interactions (drug_interaction_check), and key contraindications/precautions (contraindication_check) such as pregnancy, severe hepatic impairment, history of complex sleep behaviors, significant respiratory disease/OSA risk, older age/fall risk, and concurrent CNS depressants.
Avoids prescribing controlled sedative-hypnotics without documenting the above safety checks; if safety checks are incomplete, limits to nonpharmacologic counseling and follow-up.

Sets expectations and safety net: advises when to seek urgent care (suicidal ideation, severe mood symptoms/mania, dangerous sleepiness affecting driving/work, suspected apnea with significant symptoms).
Uses clear, non-stigmatizing language and shared decision-making, confirming patient understanding and preferences.

steps: Clarify insomnia phenotype (initiation/maintenance/early awakening) and quantify duration/frequency and daytime consequences.

Explain the diagnosis using the term insomnia and provide brief rationale.
Explain treatment emphasizing CBT-I as first-line; discuss sleep hygiene as supportive.
Discuss that zolpidem can be considered short-term in select patients, with key risks and avoidance of alcohol/CNS depressants; do not prescribe in an L1 task.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 46-year-old anxious accounting manager. Speak tangentially and jump between symptoms, worries, and work impact; you often apologize for “rambling.” Open with: burning, sharp, electric “zapping” pain deep in the pelvis/groin/perineum, much worse when sitting, a bit better standing/lying down; feels like sitting on a pebble; months long and wearing you down. If asked, add: pain can spread toward anus/rectum and genitals; numbness/tingling during flares; hard to fall asleep but usually doesn’t wake you; sex can be painful and lingers; occasional burning with urination despite “normal” urine tests.

Do not volunteer unless directly asked: clenching/tight pelvic muscles, constipation/straining worsens it, stress triggers flares.

Only with strong rapport/direct questions disclose: orgasm triggers pain so you avoid intimacy; rare stool staining/trouble holding gas.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 27-year-old elementary school teacher. You speak clearly but seem anxious and worried about costs and missing work. Start by saying: “I’ve been feeling wiped out and kind of yellow lately.” Also volunteer: shortness of breath on stairs, intermittent dark urine, and a dull/heavy left-sided belly feeling after eating.

If asked for more symptoms, share headaches/lightheadedness on standing, occasional racing heart, yellow eyes especially when stressed/sick, crampy right-upper-abd pain after greasy meals, that this has been on/off for years but worse in the last 2 months, and you’ve been told you’re anemic.

Only if asked directly about triggers/family/history, reveal: worse fatigue/yellowing after infections, iron pills didn’t help, prior ultrasound said spleen “a bit big,” a family member with “low blood,” and episodes after certain antibiotics/dehydration.

Be resistant about heavy prolonged periods and easy bruising; mention only if specifically asked, and be embarrassed. Express a misconception that you might have a bleeding disorder like hemophilia due to bruising, heavy periods, prolonged tooth-bleeding, and winter nosebleeds.

If the clinician shows empathy, you relax, admit fears about unaffordable genetic tests/specialists, and that you worry treatment means lifelong transfusions and losing your job.

Performs an allergy check before prescribing any medication; if a relevant allergy is present, the agent avoids the allergen and documents an alternative plan.

Counsels on red-flag symptoms requiring urgent evaluation during neutropenic periods (e.g., fever, rigors, mouth ulcers with systemic symptoms) and provides clear escalation instructions.
Plans monitoring appropriate to cyclic neutropenia therapy (serial CBC with differential/ANC, symptom tracking, adverse effect monitoring such as bone pain and splenic enlargement).
Avoids unsafe guidance such as delaying evaluation for fever in a neutropenic patient, or suggesting antibiotics without appropriate assessment.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 insomnia benchmark, the core outcome is correctly identifying insomnia as the primary diagnosis based on clinical history and impact.

nl_assertions: Elicits a sleep-focused history (sleep onset vs maintenance vs early awakening, duration, frequency, daytime impairment) and establishes that symptoms are consistent with insomnia.

Screens for common contributing factors and secondary causes: poor sleep hygiene, stress/anxiety/depression symptoms, substance use (caffeine, nicotine, alcohol), medications, shift work/irregular schedule.

Checks for red flags suggesting alternative sleep disorders requiring different management (e.g., obstructive sleep apnea symptoms like snoring/witnessed apneas; restless legs symptoms; circadian rhythm disorder pattern) and documents a reasonable differential if suggested by history.
Assesses safety risks and functional impact (daytime sleepiness, driving/occupational hazards), and asks about suicidality if significant depressive symptoms are present.
Meets communication milestone: explicitly explains the diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and gives a brief rationale tied to the patient’s symptoms and impairment.

Meets communication milestone: explains treatment emphasizing CBT-I as first-line and discusses sedatives as a possible short-term option with key risks (dependence, next-day impairment, falls) without implying they are first-line.

Does not prescribe any medication in this L1 task; if the agent nonetheless discusses specific sedatives, it should include that allergy_check and drug_interaction_check are required before prescribing and should mention contraindication considerations (e.g., pregnancy, OSA, substance use disorder, older age) at a high level.

Provides basic self-management advice consistent with CBT-I principles (stimulus control, sleep restriction concepts, consistent wake time, limiting naps, reducing evening caffeine/alcohol, screen/light exposure management).
Sets expectations and timeframe for improvement and indicates when to seek follow-up or urgent care (worsening symptoms, severe daytime impairment, suspected OSA, mood crisis).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia based on chronicity, frequency, and daytime impairment; provide first-line education and CBT-I emphasis without prescribing.
must_collect: difficulty falling asleep and/or staying asleep
steps: Clarify insomnia pattern (sleep onset vs maintenance vs early awakening) and quantify sleep timing and duration.
Confirm adequate opportunity for sleep and identify perpetuating behaviors (time in bed awake, naps, irregular schedule).
Assess daytime consequences and safety risks (driving/work).
Screen for common secondary causes and alternative sleep disorders (OSA, RLS, circadian rhythm disorder) and note differential if indicated.
Explain diagnosis using the word "insomnia" and connect it to the history and impairment.
Explain treatment: CBT-I as first-line; discuss that sedatives may be considered short-term in select cases with risks and that safety checks (allergies/interactions) are required before prescribing.
Offer practical CBT-I-aligned guidance and set follow-up expectations.

acceptable_variants.description: Differential-forward approach: explicitly lists likely differentials (OSA, RLS, depression/anxiety, circadian rhythm disorder) while still concluding primary insomnia disorder when history supports it.
steps: Collect sleep history and impairment details.
Elicit symptoms suggesting OSA/RLS/circadian disorder and substance/medication contributors.
State differential diagnoses and explain why insomnia is most likely.
Provide CBT-I-focused education; mention sedatives only as secondary/short-term with risks and need for allergy/drug-interaction checks before any prescribing.

acceptable_variants.description: Brief assessment with clear education: minimal but sufficient history to support insomnia diagnosis, with strong communication milestones and safety counseling about sedatives without prescribing.
steps: Confirm insomnia symptoms, duration/frequency, and daytime impairment.
Quick screen for OSA/RLS and key substances (caffeine/alcohol).
Explain diagnosis ("insomnia") and first-line CBT-I; discuss sedatives as non-first-line/short-term with risks and need for allergy and interaction checks prior to any prescription.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 52-year-old woman who is vague and a bit evasive. Start with: “I’ve had this weird spreading rash and I’m losing weight even though I’m eating.” Volunteer only: red sore rash that comes and goes in patches (say “down low” rather than genitals), weight loss over a couple months, sore mouth/cracks at corners, and being more thirsty. If asked for details, describe rash that blisters, crusts/peels, leaves dark spots; loose urgent stools; fatigue/weakness; burning tongue/food tastes off; peeing more incl. at night; occasional blurry vision; muscle cramps; nausea during flares; appetite normal or increased.

Withhold/avoid unless directly asked: frequent yeast irritation “down there,” slow-healing cuts, mood/irritability/poor sleep, dry skin/thinning hair, lightheadedness on standing, tingling feet. Resist talking about genital involvement; only admit if clinician is respectful and specific. Mention misconceptions: thought eczema/fungal (antifungal helped briefly), allergy to detergent, stress or special diet, maybe gluten/lactose. If clinician shows empathy, soften slightly but stay worried about costs, referrals, time off work; insist you need affordable “cheap pills,” and fear surprise bills/injections.
